model_output_raw_text: {"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 8","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 64","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 32","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The current model is extremely small for 256 optimization steps. A modest width increase should improve representation quality without adding architectural complexity or risking instability.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"        self.blocks = nn.Sequential(
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            nn.Conv2d(channels, channels * CHANNEL_MULT, 3, stride=2, padding=1, bias=bias),
            nn.BatchNorm2d(channels * CHANNEL_MULT) if USE_BATCHNORM else nn.Identity(),
            nn.ReLU(inplace=True),
            ResidualBlock(channels * CHANNEL_MULT, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
        )","old":"        self.blocks = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Conv2d(channels, channels, 3, padding=1, bias=bias),
            nn.BatchNorm2d(channels) if USE_BATCHNORM else nn.Identity(),
            nn.ReLU(inplace=True),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
        )","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"            nn.Linear(channels * CHANNEL_MULT, FC_HIDDEN),","old":"            nn.Linear(channels, FC_HIDDEN),","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The widened single-stage network helped, so the next step is to add a lightweight second stage with downsampling and more channels. This should improve spatial hierarchy and classifier features without a large increase in training cost.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"        self.blocks = nn.Sequential(
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            nn.Conv2d(channels, channels * CHANNEL_MULT, 3, stride=2, padding=1, bias=bias),
            nn.BatchNorm2d(channels * CHANNEL_MULT) if USE_BATCHNORM else nn.Identity(),
            nn.ReLU(inplace=True),
            ResidualBlock(channels * CHANNEL_MULT, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            ResidualBlock(channels * CHANNEL_MULT, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
        )","old":"        self.blocks = nn.Sequential(
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            nn.Conv2d(channels, channels * CHANNEL_MULT, 3, stride=2, padding=1, bias=bias),
            nn.BatchNorm2d(channels * CHANNEL_MULT) if USE_BATCHNORM else nn.Identity(),
            nn.ReLU(inplace=True),
            ResidualBlock(channels * CHANNEL_MULT, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
        )","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The current two-stage model is improving quickly. Adding one more residual block in the higher-channel stage is a targeted way to increase capacity where features are already compressed, which should be more parameter-efficient than another large width jump.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"After the architecture improvements, the optimization setup is likely under-driving the larger network. A moderate learning-rate increase is a low-complexity way to improve progress within the same 256-step budget.","secondary_modes":["summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"OPTIMIZER = \"adamw\"","old":"OPTIMIZER = \"adam\"","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"The model and learning rate are now substantially stronger than the baseline. Switching from Adam to AdamW is a simple optimizer-family change that can improve generalization for the larger residual network without changing the training loop.","secondary_modes":["caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 96","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 64","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The run is fixed by optimization steps, so increasing batch size is a direct way to expose the model to more training examples within the same 256-step budget. A moderate increase to 96 is a safer efficiency tradeoff than a jump straight to 128.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 96","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The move from batch size 64 to 96 produced a large gain, which suggests this fixed-step regime benefits from seeing more examples per update. Increasing to 128 is the most direct next test of that scaling trend.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 160","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Batch-size scaling has produced the largest and most consistent gains in this fixed-step regime. A further increase to 160 tests whether seeing even more unique examples per update continues to beat the added optimization coarseness without introducing too much extra cost.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 192","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 160","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Batch-size increases have been the clearest driver of improvement so far, and the gains have not yet saturated. Moving from 160 to 192 continues that scaling test while staying more conservative than a jump to 256.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The model is now training with much larger effective sample coverage per fixed-step run, so the original learning-rate scale may be conservative. A moderate increase to 2e-3 is a simple way to test whether the larger batch can support faster optimization without extra architectural or runtime cost.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 192","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Larger batches and the corresponding higher learning-rate scale have been the strongest improvement path in this fixed-step regime. Moving to 256 directly tests whether the sample-coverage gains continue to outweigh the loss of update noise.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 192","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The jump to batch size 256 regressed validation loss, so the scaling trend appears to have passed its sweet spot. Reverting to 192 restores the strongest visible batch setting before trying any additional refinements.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.1)","old":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"With larger batches and stronger optimization, the model may benefit from a small amount of output regularization rather than more capacity or batch scaling. Label smoothing is a compact, low-risk way to improve generalization without changing the optimizer or architecture.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 2e-4","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The current best configuration is using a larger batch, higher learning rate, AdamW, and label smoothing, which points to a regime where a bit more regularization may help generalization. A modest increase in decoupled weight decay is a simple follow-up that complements the small gain already observed from label smoothing.","secondary_modes":["indexing"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.05)","old":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.1)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"Regularization is helping, but the gains are small, which suggests the current smoothing strength may be slightly too strong for this short-horizon setting. Reducing label smoothing from 0.1 to 0.05 tests a milder version of the same idea without changing the rest of the tuned configuration.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.02)","old":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.05)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"Reducing label smoothing from 0.1 to 0.05 improved the tuned configuration, which suggests the run benefits from only mild target softening. A further step down to 0.02 tests whether the optimum is even closer to zero while keeping the regularization effect that has been helping.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.05)","old":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.02)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"Reducing label smoothing further to 0.02 regressed validation loss, so the best regularization setting found in this family remains 0.05. With one step left, restoring the strongest visible configuration is the highest-confidence move.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}
